speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Willa Cummings Carlson recalls some strange events in the past. The disappearance of Mae Downing in suspicious circumstances, an account of Winnie Booth just before she was found dead in Orifino, and the moving of a young girl's grave from a country cemetery. She also speaks of the family of Archie Mae, who was among the first pioneers of American Ridge, and with Helen Johnson recalls deaths of children from tuberculosis on Burnt Ridge.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Who were the offsprings of Dora?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Dora had no offspring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: None?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: None.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can I ask what the case was as you remember it, as it was told, what had happened then?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I hate to go into it. I don't think there's anything we want put in here. I didn't put it in the book, but Frank Mae was the richest man on American Ridge, and he had become so by having a little money. When he came here, he was a Belgian, and like J .P. Vollmer, he had profited by that way of doing, and I know that I was working there. I had to have some money so I could have some money to get a few clothes to go to teach my first term of school since I had to borrow money to go to summer school, and I had no money. They paid very poorly and had lots of work to do, and I only got $5 a week, but I went to work. I said I had three weeks that I could work in, and I'd like to have three weeks of work, so they offered me the job, and I went there to work, and believe me, I worked for it too. He came out there showing me his box, ten box of foreclosures and one thing or another. It's full of these foreclosures and go to his kids and so on, and he thought it would be so much more profitable for me to come there and work for them because they appreciated me. Just imagine, they'd give me $5 a week and work me nearly to death. And a companion to Dora, and they hadn't educated her or anything else, so she couldn't be a good companion to anybody as I saw companionship. And all this, and they would treat me so well, but if I went out to teach school, I'd have several bosses, everybody in the community would think they could teach me. So I tried to discourage me from teaching school. I said no, that's been my ambition from the time I was a little girl, and I'm going to fulfill it this year for the first time. Nothing short of death would keep me from following my ambition. Nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what happened with the harnesses?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, he had decided that he wasn't going to farm anymore, and so they rented out the land to a man named Sutton, who came in with a lot of fanfare. There was no fanfare about the May entourage, I'll tell you. There were old things fixed up with haywire, any old thing, but the machinery and so on. But they farmed however they could farm, and a little bit of wood burner plows and all that sort of thing. But Charlie Sutton had farmed out close to Moscow on rented land, and he always did it with a lot of fanfare. And my goodness, everybody looked at him with all these horses, and you know this fancy harness with the straps and the colored buckles, and the little, oh well, they were little straps. I don't know what those straps are in harnesses, I never did. I heard it, and I should know, but I don't know. But all colors of rings on it, you know, these rings. Oh, a few farmers in the neighborhood had one or two on a horse, but they didn't have them all on the harness where they were linked together. They had these straps with rings on them, harnesses. And then some of this had tassels on the hames, two arm bridles or whatever they had over. Very fancy looking, Sutton's horses were. And all his wagons were new and good looking, you know. Never seen such a show. And I guess Archie liked the looks on it. And he was man -grown. But for some reason he kind of left home and gone over to the potlatch where they had land, and he was looking after that. And eventually that's what he got for his share of the inheritance. They just got through his mother, and then they had one boy older than Dora, that was Warnie. And Warnie was Frank May's only son. He had Warnie and Dora. And Archie married a woman who had a little boy, and that little boy, and then, and then he was a little boy and she had a little girl when she married him. And it got so she couldn't stand it. And nobody really blamed her. But she, when she went of course, she took her own little girl, but she left the baby boy with a letter to Dora. She was giving her the boy. Dora had loved the little boy so much that she was giving him to Dora. And he wasn't Archie, she didn't want Archie. Archie couldn't take care of him. And so it could be Dora's. And Dora did raise water. And that's how Dora got water. And lots of people thought that Dora, or water, was her son. Her illegitimate son. Yes, her illegitimate son. This is true. Because I ran into that down at State Hospital North. Okay, well. A nurse down there asked me about that. She said, well you know that Walter was Dora's son.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She said, you know all about that, don't you?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, she, he was not. Dora was going to school out there. So I even knew Dora at the time when Walter was born. I hadn't seen him when he was little. And he was about three years old and she left him with her. So actually he was her half brother's son. Yes, he was her nephew. And he always called her Aunt Dora, which was right. But she did bring him up. She lived at home and brought him up. So of course she didn't take him around with her everywhere because she did a lot of things. And then, when finally, finally in the long run, when Frank May died and Dora, I suppose Dora was a full -fledged heir with him, warning. But it hadn't been done that way. He left her. She could run the farm as long as he lived so he could manage it with her. Dora ran the farm, of course. Seems to me she could have saved a lot of money. But after, she did hire men to run it for a while. And then she rented out after her father died. She rented out the water. Well, he wasn't much of a farmer. He didn't bring in a lot of income. And he didn't see any reason to pile up money like they had. And when fishing season opened, he went fishing. And a number of other things that had not been May customs. But Dora loved him. And he loved Dora. And so, I suppose that Dora would make a will and leave whatever she left her. Her, also Warnie had a couple of sons. And I suppose she divided up evenly among those boys. Well, but when the will was read, no, water didn't get a thing. I couldn't understand it and I was just ready to get up in arms. None of my business, you know. Absolutely none of Dora loved water. And I'm sure she left something to him. But I didn't know it all. There had been a will. But Frank May had made a will. Divided the portion that went to Archie. He was to get the land that was on the potlatch, all of it. I don't know how much. And he fiddled around and finally married a woman that got the property. She knew how. Finally met his match. And Warnie had it and Warnie was kind of a good fellow. And they say he gave Walter something, whatever Walter got out of the estate Warnie gave him. But anyway, when Dora died, and everybody suppose she lived longer than Warnie. But the estate was left to Warnie over here. All that huge estate was left to Warnie. And Dora, I don't know whether she was given any money or not. I don't know how about that. But she had no right to the property. The property hadn't been willed to her. She was just to have a live on it as long as she lived. As long as Dora lived, she couldn't be chased out of that house. That was her home as long as she lived. And she had the right to farm it. And the income from it. And the income. But only until her death. That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he really didn't treat her equally as his sons then?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh no. She wasn't treated equally. Of course not. She was a woman. You could imagine he was an old funny old fellow from Belgium. I didn't dislike old Frank Maven. He certainly was a funny old guy.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Where you were was talking about how, what was Archie stealing the bridles. And you never told me that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what was the story with that itself?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, they disappeared. One of the harnesses disappeared. Not all of it. There was some of it left. And Sutton was going to prosecute. And he told all around the country he knew who it was and he was going to catch him.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And well, the warning was going to, or Archie was going to get it out one day. And he got Howard in there in the barn so that Sutton did see him in there. But it wasn't Howard that he was trying to get. He was trying to get Archie. But then all of his telling that he knew it was Archie, Archie claimed he was in there to try to help Sutton catch Howard. Which of course he was not. And so they arrested Howard. Took him up to Moscow and Howard confessed.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Stealing the harness.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was he bought off or had he been identified?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Bought off. And they had all of the evidence. It's like I've always said in Lee's talk county, terrible. It's awful hard to get justice in Lee's talk county. I know that. But I was, my recollection might be shady so I wouldn't give it as absolute truth. But I'm pretty near right. It was the consensus of opinion of the neighbors and I heard a trial and they paid his fine and then it was a package deal and he got Dora too. She married him immediately after this fiasco. And Archie went scot free. And still the indent told. Now my Giugetta, that it was Archie that had sold him the harness.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Swore to it. So I'm told. I was told. But you know I didn't have a transcript of that trial or anything so I just stayed clear of it when it came to writing it up. But people thought that, in general, thought that Archie had done it. Oh, everybody did. Everybody did. There was no doubt in their minds. Mr. Arnold seen him so well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Archie showing it around to other people? Did he let it be known that he had done it, do you think? Why would they suspect him?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Because that was his trick. He stole everything else. And they knew if something got stolen over there at that barn it would be an inside job. I never will forget. I was a little girl. I don't know. I guess I was, this was long before the harness episode because Dora was a kid too. But I was even a little girl that had her naps in the afternoon. And my mother had told me to get my nap after dinner on Sunday. I suppose I'd eaten a big dinner because I was sleeping. It was only a lounge that we had in the living room. And I was awakened by a terrible noise. Everybody was talking and yelling and fire. And Papa came in and Mrs. Roberts was there. And Mr. Roberts and Papa came in. They were frequently came up to our place because we were right on the road. And they weren't. They were just across the road from us and down to an orchard. And they were very close neighbors. But at the same time, if they wanted to see anybody passing on the road, they'd come up to our place. And so they were both there. Mr. Roberts had been down talking to Papa. And let's see, I believe they went. No. They came up to see if they were going to go over to Frank Mays. There was a fire over at Frank Mays and they were going to go over and fight fire. And Papa said, if there's anything burning, a building burning, it's burned by now. Not much used to go. But we maybe can keep it from the house. So they went. But they laughed.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Archie and Borne had gone up to the schoolhouse. This was Sunday afternoon. And they'd gone up with a band. They used to have a band on American Ridge. And the band had met there to play for the afternoon practice. And they had gone up there. And now they were coming back. And they'd just gotten up there. And they'd been going, walked awful fast. And hadn't any more gotten up there than the boys in the band that were already there to practice said. That they all met out on the porch of the little old schoolhouse that used to be there. The one that I'd gone to first. The first schoolhouse that I went to. Just a little way from us. And they'd all met out on the porch and there. There's a fire there. Said Archie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And where's a fire?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I've heard Vince Parkins tell this and laugh. He was sending along Mr. Roberts, Lena's husband. Lena Roberts she was and she was Lena Parkins. And I've heard Vince tell us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where's the fire? Can't you see that smoke?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Just a kind of little wisp of smoke. That's over in our granary. That's coming out of our granary. Said Archie. Oh, some of them kidded him. You see smoke where there is no smoke. Yesterday he said, come on, Mornie. We've got to go and fight that fire. And away they started. Mornie of course had to do what Archie told him. And they were young men. Now I don't know. They looked grown up to me. But I suppose Archie was I imagine was about 20. I think that's what they said. But Mornie was still in his teens. He was still going to school. Still trying to get through the eighth grade. And I was under school age. So I was not six. So you know nobody trusts my memory. But I'm pretty good. What I do remember. But of course I was asleep when most of this had happened. They'd gone up past our place. And they said, better come up and hear us Charlie. That's my father. Better come up and hear us Charlie. We're going to have a good band practice this afternoon. Oh, I've got something else to do. Of course Papa always had something else to do. I've got something else to do. And Mr. Roberts was there then. I guess because I heard him say what he said to him. He told him he didn't care to go. And they went almost running to go up to the schoolhouse to get there. We're late. We had so many things to do. Archie said we're late. We're going to be late to band practice. Hurry up Mornie. And then when they got up there, why he saw this right away. Well he said you fellas come on down. And they left but here came the procession down the road. But Mornie and Archie were leading it. And people were, they were all walking. Or at least most of them were. They were all walking. Come on down help us fight fire. He yelled back at them. So they were all stringing along the road. And I can remember that procession coming along the road. Men, the band boys they called them. Playing band. And there it is, there it is coming up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how do you know it's the granary?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh I know it's the granary. It's right there. Archie had told him. He knew the location of the granary. They had big buildings over there. Looks more like the barn to me someone had said. Well it's kind of back of the granary. Granary's out in front. Oh that's the granary alright. He knew exactly where it was. So they got over there. Of course they didn't get over there in time to save the granary. Tiger and Roush. Adjust yourself to circumstances. Yes I have. I have I'm very glad I was that way too. Yes. Yeah I think a lot of burnt ridge. So when I think about the ridges I think of burnt ridge and American Ridge. And I learned all I could learn about them as I did. Now I don't know what about you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You don't remember any other place do you than burnt ridge?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No I was born and raised there. That's what I was thinking that you were born there. In 1892. 1892. So you see she's. Now you can figure out how old I am. I think it was 1892. I never can remember years. Well I was born in 1896. So I had four years on the century. I always say that that I had four years on the century. You're four years younger than I am then. I guess so yeah.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Johnson what was burnt ridge like when you were growing up there?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: My hearing isn't too good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was burnt ridge like when you were growing up there?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well I can tell you the worst I guess.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: It had seen its worst days by the time I got old enough to remember you know I mean the old pioneer days. Well it still was you know. But by the time I was old enough to remember anything. Why it had improved a lot. Oh yes. Yes by the time I was old enough. Well I don't suppose you always lived in that house. No. Your father had that built later. Yes. The house we had. Dad had. Our parents had. It was a quarter. A little more than a quarter of a mile and not as much. Quite as much as a half a mile further west. Further back. That was the folks' home for that house. And this house that we lived in by the road now you know. The place where we lived. It'll be 72 years now. It is 72 years this summer. Oh. Since it was built. Since it was built. Oh my. You see and that was their very second house and it's quite a big house. A very nice roomy house for the time you know. Yeah it was. But of course now this is old fashioned. Well it's old fashioned but it's a nice house yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did your parents come out?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well my father came out in 1884 from Ashtabula, Ohio. He had been there three years but he came from Sweden to Ashtabula, Ohio. And he was there three years in Ashtabula, Ohio before he came to Troy. And now let's explain to him one thing about Ashtabula. You see the people who settled Burnt Ridge were a lot of them were either related or were good friends. And they didn't come from Minneapolis like nearly everybody else had. They came from Ashtabula, Ohio. And that was the place where, well that was the center of that Lutheran branch. You see Westbrooks came from there and the Westbrooks. Carl and Hill, my son and I was parents. And Paul Smith you know that would be grandparents of Paul and Clarence. And Carl Anderson, Ruth Kelberg's uncle. And Olaf Johnson, they came from Ashtabula and died. See there were an awful lot of people who came from Ashtabula so they settled there and they were almost like relatives because they lived close together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did it have to do with the church?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well they were Lutherans. They were from the same branch of the Lutheran church. Ashtabula is one of the sources if you read up the Lutheran history. Well you see they were Lutherans originally from Sweden you see. Yes they were all from Sweden. Naturally they got a church going of their own branch. And I don't know the different branches but there was a difference in some because they had little different rights. They're the same church and same beliefs but just a little different customs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your parents tell you what Burnt Ridge was like when they first came out?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh yes I guess they talked about that so many times but I don't remember all of it. It was nothing but wilderness. Timber and grass and brush. That's all it was. And the place your husband's parents had Joseph Carlson. Somebody else had been there before. But I don't remember who that was. It had been settled by somebody else. Jack Driscoll had homesteaded it and had the homestead rights on it so they bought out his homestead right. Or his homestead Paul's father bought from him clear down on the point of the ridge. And there were a number of things there. Paul's father had been a carpenter and he worked away from home and it was a place to get his boys so he put the boys to work to break up the land for him. The doll had to be broken up so my husband and his oldest brother they were set to work doing that. And the next two boys got to go to school. And they were sent away to school. And of course it was never quite fair. Walter didn't think it was fair. And Walter the oldest one left home and went to Spokane to go to business college later on. Got a business education and then from that time on he went over into Montana and homesteaded himself over there. And then he became a pioneer. But he didn't marry and he just stayed there long enough to get the crop in and he'd leave. And in the meantime in the wintertime he got a job in bookkeeping someplace. Because that's where he'd gone to business college in Spokane. So that was the way the Carlson family had done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you folks came to American Ridge?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh I was only three.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Three?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what are some of the things that they tell about in the early days in Burnt Ridge? Do you know some of the things they tell about what happened there?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: He wants to know if you remember some of the stories that they tell. They just tell if you've heard them tell about in the early days what did we do and how different was it now to what it was then. That I don't remember because you know they would talk about that when they get together. And I'd forgotten that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When I worked you somehow related to the Larsons?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yeah. August Larson was mother's brother. My mother and August Larson were brother and sister. I see. But they had four children but there's only one left, Amanda. Oh. And well, Willa remembers Amanda. Yes, I remember Amanda. I remember, I knew Amelia and Lars too of course. Oh yes, sure you would. I remember them.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And Agnes is the only one I didn't know.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: But I heard about her so much because Agda or Agnes, which was it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Agda. Agda was the youngest one. A -B -D -A. She passed away when she was ten. And I think, I don't think you was married at that time. I didn't know her and the only way that I knew about her and knew about it so well was that the Carlsons talked about it. They had, Evelyn was in that age as a baby. Oh yeah, Carlson's younger. Carlson's Evelyn. And then the little girl of Larson's and there were some others in that age group. That could be. See this Evelyn, Paul's younger sister, she was only a few months when she passed away. Well, she was sixteen months old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: months old? Fifteen months old?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Sixteen months old. Sixteen. Well, you see, the Larson was ten years old. Yeah, I know she was older. Yes, she lived to be older, I mean. But she actually was the same age you lived. Yes, that's it. They were born at the same time, the same year. Fever that swept, I think. Which you can ask.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there some kind of a fever that was killing?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No, they didn't all die the same thing. They died at different times, like she said. One little girl, Evelyn Carlson, Paul's sister, my husband's sister, was only sixteen months old. And they thought she was a very bright child and she was so cute and they just couldn't get over it. But they said all about these children, but they were in the same age and something happened to them before they were grown. A whole group of them. But it wasn't the same thing. It just seemed strange. There was one of the Kelbergs, I don't know which one it was. Minnie. Minnie, yes, that's right. Minnie Kelberg. And they called it in that time consumption. They call it TB now. And so did Dr. Larson. That was consumption. Or they call it TB now. Yes, it was the same thing. Yes. Yes, and they were in that age group.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the grown -ups in pretty good health? Was it just the children that were affected?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, they were all in pretty good health, but they did have some TB on the ridge. The family came in, supposedly, and brought TB into the community. I don't know who. I believe they said the Pearsons brought it to the ridge. I don't know. And some in Kelberg. You know, Hilma Kelberg had it later than Minnie. And Hilma was older than Minnie. Yes, she didn't have it then. But Mrs. Aller had it. Yeah, that's right. And Mrs. Aller was the sister of Mr. Kelberg. Not too long ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, you say it was through the school, through the kids going to school?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, that's what it was generally supposed to be.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: That's what did it. Oh, by the way, I had a letter from Edith not too long ago, and she said that Amy... Pearson. Pearson. Was very low. Yes, she has, she has, you know, she's been in a nursing home for quite a few years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, was she in a nursing home?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yes, she has been, so she's still there. But they have, she's been, I guess her mind has been wandering too, but she isn't able to be up. She hasn't been, I don't know how she is now, able to be up to speak up. She's in bed most of the time. She's been for a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see Amy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, anyway, or Mavis, Edith said that, that, what's it, Jo. Jo Pearson. Jo and Edith were with her all the time. Oh, well that could mean, you see, something worse maybe, you see. She said she was worse. Yes, I see. Jo and Edith were with her all the time. Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that is all, isn't it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Jo and Edith and Amy. Yes. That's what she said about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If people got, or youngsters got consumption, was there any hope for them? Once they got tuberculosis?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Not in that early day, no. There wasn't. They eventually died. Some lasted it out quite a while. But they eventually died of it in that day. Now, Hilma Kilbert, they discovered it in her, and they thought they could keep her. They sent her to a sanitarium in Denver. And it didn't save her. No. She was down there quite a long time. Yes, she was. And then she came back home and lived for at least two, three years after she left Denver.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she live that long ago?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: She lived quite a long time after she came home. But anyway, they told her there wasn't any hope. They couldn't save her. And the Swanson girls, they couldn't save them either. No, that was Hilma and Lida. Hilma and Lida.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, who were they? Were they Ed Swanson's sisters?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: They were Alvin's sisters. Oh, oh. They were younger than Alvin. I guess Hilma was next to Alvin in order, in age. And Lida next to him. And then Ethel was the youngest. Yes, and Ethel just died. Now, when they were girls on the ridge, I thought, honestly, I thought Ethel just looked terribly bad. And I thought that Ethel was worse off than the other two girls. Well, then Hilma died. And of course, we knew she wasn't worse off than Hilma. But I thought she was worse off than Lida. But my goodness, that's been a long time ago since Lida died. But Ethel just died last winter. It was a year ago in February.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a year ago?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: In February. The first part of February. I'll tell you, they were pretty healthy. And there were not a great many children that died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Not a great many. There were a few. Now, Kellebergs, they had this big, big family. Awful big family. And of course, TB got into it. And there were ravages of different things. But they had little Boyd. Oh, the youngest one. The youngest one who died. He was four years old. Boyd. Boyd. He was four years old. And he had, what the doctor finally said, he was a long time diagnosing it, that it was spinal... No, no. It was... Well, it was meningitis of the brain.

speaker: Sam Schrager

words: And so they... All those women here were great scrubbers, I tell you. Yeah.

words: That's something I don't remember.

words: I don't think so. I was thinking that they were moving all the cemetery, but everybody had been buried there. And they took it out, and they wanted to open the casket. And I remember. Her father, I was saying, was her father insisted on opening the casket. There were several men working out there. Well, it seemed to me that it was old Grandpa Larson, as they called him, and so it might have been a sister. Well, August wasn't an old grandpa. He wasn't as old as Paul's father, any of them. Maybe it was his father. I think it was his father that insisted it, and it was August's sister. I'm kind of thinking it was that. But I'm not sure anyway. It was a girl by the name of Larson would be as far as I dare go. I wouldn't dare tell which family she belonged to. But it was the Larson family, and it was the same Larson family that Helen's mother was a Larson, see. And I think it was. I think it was. It would have been a sister to Mrs. Johnson, probably. Anyway, they opened the casket. And, uh, I don't remember just how long she'd been dead, but she'd been dead quite a long while. And it withered out there anyway. I don't know what, it was more than two years. And, my goodness, she had, her cheeks were pink, and she looked like a, she looked so natural. And, my goodness, they were all startled there. Yeah, the men didn't want him to open the casket. The father. And they tried to keep him from doing it. Just moved the casket, and he insisted on prying it open. And they found her in the perfect condition. Because they thought it would be too frightening. You know, he really couldn't stand seeing her all decayed and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did they think?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, wasn't it, they didn't think something well. Yes, they thought that. They said, well, my goodness, I remember different theories expressed. They were very blond, the Larsons were. And, uh,

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: and that they, this was the red cheek. And that she must have been alive. And, of course, I knowing now what I know, I don't believe that at all, of course. But they thought, and that she'd been buried alive. Well, some men had sense enough to say, no. Anything like that, there'd have been gases, you know, naturally.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Where they're alive, when they're buried. And the gas, natural gases just simply burst a coffin. It does happen. Sometimes a person is buried alive. But there's all kinds of destruction brought about by it. That was pointed out by people who happened to know. Paul said, Daddy knew, he heard all about this. I don't know what he was there. It seems to me like maybe Daddy was there helping move the cemetery. I'm not sure what he was doing, but. Well, speaking of, uh. But, she asked him as the air got to him, she fell to pieces. So, they went and opened the casket again, and of course she was, she fell to dust. That's what happens. And then somebody, they kind of decided, oh, she'd been buried next to a water vein or something. Yeah, there was a water vein, a vein of water that ran right past it. And that was ice cold water. Because it ran off the hill back of it. It was terribly cold. And it kept it in perfect condition, being underground and no air getting to it. But as soon as they opened the casket, air entered it. And the second time they looked at it, it was just, it was just nothing. They had just all fallen to dust around the bones.

words: Well, I saw that he almost went nuts. No, I didn't know that. It takes a lot to put a sweet in, nuts.

words: Well, why?

words: It seems to me like I remember that. I don't remember that. I don't know. Of course he was shaken. They were all shaken. Some of them didn't want to prowl around the cemetery anymore.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: They used to always be telling stories. I heard several stories about people being buried alive. That I know now, as a grown person, and know about conditions, I know couldn't have been true. But what I got was that he, the father, took it as a sign from God, though, that she had been chosen and was select and special. Oh, I don't think that. I don't know. I don't know, but I never heard that. No, it was a buried alive theory, and that's what Paul's mother was always talking about. She wondered. She wondered because she was a little bit susceptible, former or less. She wondered if she could have been buried alive.

words: Well, if it were a Larson girl, it would have had to have been that one. Unless that was one of August's daughters. Yes, but if it were this family. But if it was August's sister. Well, I just knew it was a Larson girl, and I knew it was Old Man Larson. That's what they said. I didn't remember if it was August or August's father. Well, I don't know who it was.

When you leave here, because I think too much of you to have the same thing happen to you that happened to Winnie Booth when she stayed down here on this bench. No other teacher, I guess, had ever boarded down there since Winnie had. Because, you see, I lived down there on the bench. Way down there was a lot going down at the bottom of the canyon to go up to my school every day. My folks didn't want me to stay there. That waits, but thank goodness I never was better in my life, I mean healthier, than I was that year, and I loved it there. I didn't mind the climb. Of course, it was always nice to get a ride up the hill. And Mrs. Elliott was not my landlady, but she lived close there. It was when she came up there to see how I was getting along with them when they were all sick of the flu. But me, and it was kind of lucky to have me around. I knew how to do a few things. I even baked bread for them on Saturday. You know, and I cooked for them, and they didn't need much to eat. They were all sick of the flu.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: But she said that that day Winnie had, I don't know that she was going to come, I don't remember this. Here's a link that I've left out. That was it. And that was it, because here she said, came Winnie down in the evening after her school was out with this old dentist. Doctor.

But anyway, here he came in a rubber tired buggy, you know, and a fancy driving team. Came driving Winnie down to her boarding place and she said she wanted to pick up a few things. That she was going away for the weekend. Mrs. Elliott thought she was going home for the weekend. So she got these few things and went. She kissed Mrs. Elliott goodbye. And she said she just felt bad after they'd gone off. And she said to her husband that she didn't like the looks of that man a bit. No, he said he didn't either. Understood it was a married man. Come on here after Winnie and he didn't like it at all. And the next day the news was out. They didn't go to Moscow at all. They went to Orifino. Took a room at the hotel.

I know Mavis is very small when she's taking in a lot of the stories that she's heard. And I was the same way about all those stories. And I heard all about Winnie Booth. I never forgot the name of Winnie Booth. Then my goodness after I was married, Paul told about Winnie Booth had been his Sunday school teacher. And he and all the other little boys, they talked about when they got big they were going to marry a girl that looked like Winnie Booth. And Winifred was his favorite name. Mavis came almost being Winifred. I certainly wish I had been.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: So she was rather pretty.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: According to Paul, she was a beautiful girl. I never saw her.

words: Elliott say that Winnie said anything to her when she said goodbye?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I recall when you talked to me about it before, you mentioned her saying that she had said thank you.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: He'd gone to Arefino and that was it. Well, he's kind of taken care of it himself. Yes. Well, that talk is interesting. Even though it's just hearsay, it just tells you what people were thinking at the time. Yes, well they were thinking and believe me, they were furious with that man.

words: But my story is covered by the fact that Carol Ryrie Brink...

words: Mentioned, but nothing more than mentioned?

words: And what in the Buffalo Coat was true and what was not? What was just added?

Well, I do remember that Elsie Watkins herself, who's going to be quite an oldish woman, and I got her down there and she was crippled with rheumatism. She had an awful time moving around. But people aged, as I say, younger in those days.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: She said that Winnie was an awful sweet girl. And this old coot that killed her...

words: Well, there's a boy mixed up in it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I'd better get into that. I'll mix them again. Her mother.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: This old doctor seemed to try to bewitch the women no matter what age.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: So she based her observations on the fact that she knew what he was. He chased the women. Older women.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: But he and his wife didn't have any children of their own.

words: I never did find out. And I have asked a lot of places.

words: What were the grounds for her telling that there was a boy in the buggy with Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Watkins?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Watkins, the day he was killed. Yes.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: That was by somebody entirely different. So that's mixing two cases together. One has to be careful not to do that. But there was a little thread of things going through it. speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to his wife? The dentist's wife?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, she was outraged. I asked that. And was told that she was outraged.

words: She went back east to her people. They had come from the east here. And I remember that Aunt Elsie said that. But she just went back east to her people. And she disposed of everything they had here which didn't amount to much. This whole rate I suppose it spent it all.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: When we were talking before, you talked a little bit about the madam and the hotel in Troy. And you said that she really helped people. Helped people out. And this intrigued me. Nobody ever called her madam, the one in Troy.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: As far as I know they hadn't gone that far so they didn't call them. But those Hayes women who had gone to Lewiston, they were called madam. They wanted to do it because this Frenchman that had married one of them I think. I think that's where they got that idea. Madam. No, this woman's name was Mrs. Mosher. I guess as far as I have a sister who married into a Mosher family. But it's certainly not that Mosher family. But later on I don't know. That Mosher family as far as I know died out with them. With them. But yes, she was a big hearted woman. When somebody would be sick she'd take them to that hotel and take care of them. I've heard people say she fed lots of people and didn't have anything to eat. And they came there and she'd see to it they got something to eat. Unless she did. It was very good to people like that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: This is all ears. I don't know about what she did to them but I know she was big hearted. Did things because I know she used to come out and rage to visit people. But she knew.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: So it really wasn't minded too much that she kept a house.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No. No.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Funny enough I don't think it's talked about too much about she doing it. But I knew quite a number of people talked about it. And every time she'd come visiting on the ridge to visit people she knew. They would tell about it. Her daughter married one of the Parkers men. Of course they were, they drank like fish. And one of these Parkers married Nina Sanders. One of these Sanders girls. And that Nina Sanders was a bosom friend of my husband's sister Ellen. And they were a very very good friend. I didn't know Nina Sanders. They lived on this other ridge that was Burnt Ridge. And you didn't go back and forth to those ridges. 26 miles around by. Horse and buggy you know. And I never went around there nor my husband ever came around. In those days. But Nina and Ellen were very good friends. And Nina Sanders married an older brother of these Parkers. And they lived up at Orophena. And you know Nina died at a fairly early age. I know I cut the clipping out and sent it to Ellen. And she wrote back and said she got the word before I even sent that. As quick as it was. That I think it was Eva or Eva Nelson that knew it. I believe it was Eva that called her and told her. Because she thought Ellen would want to know it. Knew they were very good friends. But she just, this sister that visits Edith. The young one, the playmate to Edith and Bud. That was Nina's younger sister. One of the Sanders.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: She was there at the time visiting Nina. And they'd done up the work and then I said they'd have to get a meal for the men.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I don't know whether her younger sister, what was her name?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Edith's friend, Merle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because Bud used to say, Can Dita and Ditta go play with Ma?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And I said when I heard him say that, I said, well my goodness, that's another source of where maybe when she started to talk, she used the first syllable. I said probably me because I remembered that I did that a lot. Used the first syllable. They'd tell about the way I talked. I used the first syllable and doubled it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And made Mr. Carlson so mad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell them about something about how they had to get a meal for the men? Was that what you said?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Had to get a meal, well you see, these Germaines, Merle, Sanders, much, much younger than Nina, they were visiting in Oropheno with Nina and her husband. And her husband was Charles Portfurs. And he was an older brother of the man who had married the oldest daughter. No, not the oldest daughter, but a daughter, one of the older daughters of this woman that had ran the hotel in Troy. It was always called the hotel, but a lot of people would smirk and say things about this hotel.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And Charlie Portfurs, I saw him after he got to be an old man. I met him. Paul always talked to him. He was one that could have told you some startling tales about Troy's young days. He probably would have told them. But anyway,

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Merle was there when Nina said that to her, well she'd sit down and rest a little and then they'd have to get the meal. Yes, get the meal ready. Get the noon meal ready as I remember it. Oh, big, Lewis and Treban had almost full size sheets of Nina Portfurs. She was a very fine woman and she'd done lots of things. She belonged to this lodge and that lodge and was at the head of it and took care of a lot of poor people around Orophenia. They did lots of things. I think if you'd asked the old timers in Orophenia now, they'd remember Nina Portfurs. Well, of course Portfurs is a big name in Orophenia. Yes, they're still a Portfurs. That's why I had Portfurs Jones in the bottle gown.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have mail order brides very much?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Not much here. Not much here, but we heard of them in different places. But there wasn't much here. I think it was Blakey.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Maybe there still. Asked me if Grandma was a mail order bride. No. Somebody said so. Grandpa. He married me on a ride. And I said no. However, she'd been wanting to get away from back there in Missouri where she was.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: She had some very good friends here. They don't live around this country at all anymore.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Not even his other brother. One more story that I wanted to ask that we didn't put down and that you told partly before. That was about the disappearance of May Downing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, didn't you get that?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Uh -uh. I've gone through all that, I know. Now I want to finish this about Grandpa since I mentioned it. I wouldn't have that get down wrong for anything in this world. There was one of the kids that heard this story. Somebody said, well, talking about mail order bride. Your grandpa, that's the way he married your grandma. Somebody told me this. One of the kids I don't know might not be one of yours, but it seems to me it was. I don't think so because I haven't heard it. Oh, maybe not. Maybe it's one of the others. Anyway, one of my own grandchildren.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And so she came out. They told her that she could get good places to work out here. And George Walker told her that he would, if she didn't have the money, he would loan her the money to come on. And get a place to work out here. She could make so much more money than she could back in Missouri where they didn't have anything. And she came out to see Aunt Gertie. Now this Aunt Gertie is no relation whatever to us, but she wanted me to call her Aunt Gertie. And Orville said because she was the only... Well, she was a person I knew mama best, I guess. Mama and Mrs. Walker were very good pals, very good friends. So mama came out there and grandpa worked at Thomas's right around there. And he and George worked together on that farm. And he boarded, I don't know why, but he boarded down there most of the time, George Walker's. And they soon did marry. It wasn't too long afterwards. Soon afterwards. But that was the story. And it did not have in it any mail order. However, George Walker had told her that he had a fellow picked out for out here. Paula.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, where did George Walker live? Missouri or out here?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: They had lived in Missouri, a new mother. Oh, I see. And then he had come out here and he landed down there where everybody did. Down on that end of the ridge working for Thomas's. And papa was there. Papa was there, man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He had worked and then he made his home at Al Roberts's, see?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I see. And he made his home there to come back up every now and then. But he actually boarded up Walker's. And George Walker's. And he and George became good friends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they were out here a couple or three years ahead of mama, see?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, that's the story. It wasn't much of a story. I'm sorry about that. Well. There was one thing I wanted to know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any tie -up between the dentist and Winnie Boo and the paupers that married the Mosher girl?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: None that I know of. Oh, okay. Well, somehow it went into that and I thought there was. Yeah, we've written the stories together and talking about them here. You might possibly get to thinking they're related with stories, but they're not. And that dentist, I can't think of his name, but it seems to me he had... It was a French name, it seems to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is he French or Italian?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And he was a dark man, very dark hair and dark eyes. I remember he was picturing big old bug eyes, bugged out eyes, you know. That kind of eyes. Looked like quite a desperado, really.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, well how about the disappearance of Mae Downing?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, the Downing family came here and they lived what we call up above Troy, about two miles. It was that house, you know, up there almost opposite the school. It was opposite the school, I believe, they had up there. Fairly good looking house as you go out toward Big Meadows, but not very far up into it. And I believe it's still there with the red roof and red trim on the house. Well, they came out, this family, Downing's. Mr. Downing was kind of a cripple, I don't know what, but I think about how he was, kind of a rheumatic or arthritic cripple. He used a cane all the time, wasn't able to do anything, but he did write some. Did some writing, of course, Clara always said. And they had a family of girls, I think it was five. Let's see, it was, and they all came there, when they first came, they all came there.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Ida, is the oldest one I think, Ida and what was the other one?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I suppose I've had that so long that I've forgotten it. Ida, and as far as I know, Ida's still living. And then the one that married, was married soon after they came or married when they arrived. And she's the one that was married to this George something or other and they lived over on the coast. I'm short on names now, I knew all these at one time. Well, it doesn't. But there were the two older girls, Ida and May, it was Ida and May. And then this girl that was married, and she was married to George, you know, he's the one that, she lived over at Portland, they never did live up here. She'd come to visit, but they lived over at Portland or near Portland or something like that. In Portland I think. And Clara used to go down and visit them once in a while. And then there were the two young girls. And they seemed to be younger than all three others, but then they weren't so much older than they were either. And that was Clara, or Kelly they called her, Kelly. Everybody called her Kelly, but me, and they said when they brought her here, or when they came here, it was, she registered to school as Clara Downing. And Babe Downing, the youngest one, was called Babe. She had another name too, but I never got onto that because she wasn't in any class of mine, neither was Clara. Except one, there was some class she was in after a while. Clara, I was kind of attracted to her, so I always knew Clara, met her right away. And it was Clara and Babe, they were the two young ones. But Ida and May were the two older ones. Yes, that's five year olds. Well, after a few years, Clara got to going with a boy out in American Ridge close to us, and a very good friend of my brother's. I have his picture someplace. Marilyn Cheney and Orville taken together their picture. I don't know, I'm sure it's in the album someplace. And, uh, Marilyn married Clara. Well, Ida married a fellow in Spokane, and May went up there to work. Well, Clara, of course, wanted May to be along with her, and she was always angling for her. Or to go with May, always. And he went with her some. Papa said she was too old for him, she was older than Orville, and he was very much against this. And May wasn't the colorful lady that Clara was. Clara, I thought, was quite colorful, and she was good looking, and she made up well, and she looked so nice, and she was dressed up much better than May, but May did seem to be a lovely girl. She was a very nice girl. And Ida was a schoolteacher, and she went to teaching. In the summertime she'd come up to Spokane and get some job around some restaurant there, usually as a cashier, because she was good at that. And May used to go up there and work with her. And finally one summer, she went up there, it wasn't so long after Downings had come here, she went up there and Ida was married to Ralph, something or other.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And Ida said, Ralph and I are going to get you married this time. So they got her introduced to this Walter Stevenson.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And am I going to say, she did get married, and she didn't know him too awful long, I don't know just how long, but she didn't know him too long, and she married Walter Stevenson. Well, they all were a great family to be together, the girls, and they'd all be together a lot. And Clara said they always were together a lot, and they used to have Kodak pictures taken of the family, and one thing another, and Walt was a clown, and he'd go and, he was a small fellow, she described him, I never saw him, and he'd run around from one to the other and pop behind him and then out again, and when the picture snapped, and then hide, and when they went to look for a picture, they didn't have a single sign of a picture of him, Kodak picture, he'd avoided them all. But they hadn't thought about it at the time. But I don't know how long they were married, but it was four or five years. And in that time, Clara goes sick. And May said, well, now that Marvin can't work out on the farm, you come up to Spokane and stay with us here. The apartment's big enough for you and Marvin to come up and live in this apartment. They could get another, oh, I think they got another room or two to add to it. Apartments were more plentiful at that time. And they had the baby, Billy was the baby. So they went up there to stay that year. And of course, Walter was gone. And then May said it was so nice to have Clara there, so that they wouldn't be alone. She just hated to be alone all the time. Walter was always gone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his business? Do you remember that?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, he was supposed to be a bond salesman and deal in, and his aunt had left him a fortune. And he had to manage his aunt's property. And he was constantly going to Chicago to take care of his aunt's property.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: So, they never did know exactly what he was doing. But he had been a bond salesman, so he claimed.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: He'd been a decent provider, as far as May was concerned. Oh, yes. Apparently so. I never heard Clara, and she would have been the first one to say it. I never heard her say anything about the way it provided. I think that everything was provided all right. But after a while, I don't know whether it was next year or not, but soon after that, May said that Walter was going to be gone a long while. And she was going to go down and stay with Lula, Lila. That was that other girl, Lila. She was going to go and stay with her. But she wanted to visit Clara before she went to visit Lila. So, she was going to come down here. And so, she brought a great big trunk, all locked in. Oh, it was a heavy thing, Clara said. And she said, I didn't know what in the world to do with that trunk. She said, Walter told me that I had to take it with me wherever I went, that I was to take that trunk. So, I had to bring it down here, and when I go down to Lila's, I've got to take it down there. So, Clara thought it was an awful nuisance, as she said. And she's very outspoken. So, she didn't much like the idea of having that trunk around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's in it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, May didn't know what was in it, and she said, I don't care. Well, I'd care. Because Marvin's trunk, I'd open it. I wouldn't keep a trunk, because I didn't know what was in it. And she talked that way all the time to see her. And May said, well, you don't need to get into that trunk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she said, do you know?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I said, no. No, she didn't. Clara told me that they had that kind of a conversation several times. And Clara would be persistent about a thing like that, because that was her way.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, finally, they got a telegram from George, Lila's husband, saying that she was sick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And if May was coming, wouldn't she come now and stay with her?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Because she didn't want to stay. George had to leave for a few days. And his wife didn't want to be alone. She thought it would be nice to have her there. So, she got ready. She said, yes, she'd come down. She got ready to go, and of course, the trunk had to be taken down and mailed to, expressed, down to Portland. So, May in the trunk left Clara's. And I don't know now for time, but not too long afterward. There was some little thing. She had had words. She'd had a telephone call from him, from Chicago, while she was at Clara's. And wanted her, we sent her a document, and he wanted her to go down to Troy and sign it before an ordinary public. And it was for the disposal of a hotel. And she did it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And sent it to him, whatever she got. That was the only thing she heard, the only thing of any significance that Clara sent it. She seemed to hear from Walter. And I don't remember her hearing from him, but I think a card known then. Wasn't much. Well, now, yeah, this seemed like you were on the phone, Mark. But, was that property in Chicago that was... Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...that they disposed?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yes. I see. But he sent the thing to her, but she had to have her name on it. And she had to go before an ordinary public and sign it. And it was a lot of money. I don't remember what the amount was, but it was a lot of money. Well,

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Lila was down there and wasn't down there very long. Until one night there was a telephone call. After 10 o 'clock, it came to George's. And George answered, and he said it was Walter.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And one night May was there, and he said, Well, George, will you get ready and take her down to the railroad station?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: He said, I want her to meet me. It has to be tonight. Oh, George said, Surely that night's storming. It's just terrible. He said, I can't leave long. He said, I don't want to leave my wife alone, but for very long. Well, he said, Get somebody else then or she can come in a cab. We'll go down to the railroad station. He told her which one in Portland. And he said, I want her to meet me at such and such a place. And I used to remember that name, but I remember now. But it was a little suburb of Portland. I want her to meet me there.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, he said, Wait just a minute. You talked to May. And he said to May, I don't know if that's Walter or not. I don't know. He said, You talk to him and find out.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: So she went to the telephone, Oh, Walter, is that you?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And she was just overjoyed to hear him. She said, Why, certainly she'd come there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then when she got off the line, Walter says, Now do you know, or I mean George, do you know that that was Walter talking?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, yes, she said, I know his voice anyplace. Of course it was Walter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What makes you say such a thing?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, he said, I didn't think he talked right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, What in the dickens does he want you to meet him out there?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: That's Godforsaken place for. It's just a little bit of a suburb. Walter said, I know the place well. And I don't know why he'd want you to meet him there on a rainy night. And he said, I don't think there's a thing there, but just a little shelter. Of course there's lots of those, used to be around the Sound out there. Question as to whether Walter was actually calling from the place that he said he was. Yes, yes. And he immediately put a call in and asked where that call was from. At Central. And it was, and he got the word. You don't always know when you do that. But he got the word back that it was, uh, it was Portland Station. Portland Payphone. Portland Payphone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he had said that he was calling from someplace else, didn't he?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yeah, he said this little town outside of Portland. And Maeve got angry with him for having the call traced. Oh yes, she was maddering it in. Walter said he was there, that's where he was. Oh yes, she didn't like anything that George did. Well, George talked to his wife, and he said to her, I don't like it, I don't like it. He said, if you were better, stronger, I would go with Maeve out there. She didn't want him to leave her either. And I guess they didn't. I don't remember, I don't see why they couldn't have gotten somebody to stay with her. But as far as I know they didn't. And he took Maeve down and when he...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With the trunk, right?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: With the trunk, yes. That was part of the orders. Oh yes, to bring the trunk. And where they sent the trunk, I don't know, I don't think Claire knew. But anyway, when I talked to her, I don't know, they did. So they went down there and he took this precaution, George did. He got on the train to look for Walter. And he went through the entire train. And he asked the conductor. He wasn't on the passenger list. They don't have the passengers down, I don't know why they had any. They've always said that he wasn't on the passenger list. But at least they didn't know of anybody that... Of that description that was there. Didn't hear any word of him at all. And he said, Maeve, I hate to leave you, without leaving, without finding him. Oh, she said he's... Oh, she had some excuse for him. Probably worried about the trunk. About to get the trunk. Well, he wasn't out to get the trunk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean George went clear with her to the suburb?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yes. To put her on the main train in Portland and to go to the suburb. Just to go to the suburb. That was all. But he was just sure she was in there. That he would come in there. But he wasn't there. No, he didn't go out to the suburb. He didn't want to leave his wife. He just put her on the train. So when he got home, he asked when I had hit that suburb and he knew when the train had hit there. And he called up and asked if the man answering that description had gotten off the train and he knew that there'd be some few get off it. You know, at that time of night in Edelgill Station. They know. And there was no one that answered that description that got off there.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Or had gone to meet it, maybe. He might have gone to meet it there, too. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People telling him what?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, they didn't know. Bubble heads. I just thought it was awful. I thought they should have found out, but then maybe I'd have been just as dumb. Especially when you have to pay money, you know, to get... People aren't always long on cash, you know, to get things done. I don't know, but I always felt that it was certainly a foolish thing that nobody learned about that. Well, at first... It was the last year, you see. Last time. And they never got any word from her. Nor of all. No. Never a word, never a line. And Clara had addressed an envelope to her home for Mae to write to her when she got there. Because she knew how careless Mae was about carrying letters around. Clara did that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And I don't know when they told her to telephone him. George said, you telephone me from there. And if something happens you can't telephone me tonight, you telephone me the first thing in the morning. And if something had happened, he thought he just had a hunch Walter might prevent it. I want to prevent it. Said, you telephone me. And she promised him faithfully that she would. Well, they never heard from her. They never saw her. They don't know a thing. That's the last that anybody saw Mae Downing.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Now, she was not a beautiful girl, but she was nice looking. And always nicely dressed. She wasn't beautiful. She wasn't a startling looking girl. Rather plain, I thought Mae was. Very nice girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But does George always had, her brother -in -law always had a funny feeling about him?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Always did. He always had had. He'd always had. She made that remark to him. George, you never have trusted Walter. No, he said you bet he hadn't.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: But his wife insisted he, you know, not say anything nor to Mae about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I said, did you ever do anything about him?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yes, she said that Ida's husband, who was an up and coming fellow, they went back to Chicago. And he says, well, he used to work at such and such a place. So he went there to see if he'd been there. They hadn't heard from him in years. They didn't know where he was. But they heard him. They knew he'd gone some other street in Chicago and might be over there. They might know. So he did follow up two or three leads as to where he might be. But he left every one of the places. They all knew him. But he wasn't at any of them. And so he went down to the Department of Missing Persons and registered him.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, have you got a picture?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No, he didn't have a picture of any kind. But Ralph, to come from the rest of them or Mae, but Ralph it seems, had had an idea where he and Walter had been together. Ralph was a meat cutter. He always had a good job at some big shopping center where he could be a meat cutter.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Was that Ida's husband?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Ida's husband.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And Walter, what did he do?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: He was cashier or something always around the hotel. He was around these big eating places too. Seen.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And he had a notion that they'd been in a big group picture once together. He got that, but it was very poor and indistinct. Clara said you couldn't have told much by it, she didn't think. She wouldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know if Mae had the key to the trunk?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No, I don't think she did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she didn't know what was in it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, she didn't know what was in it. She didn't know what was in it. And when he sent for her to get that paper signed, he sent her the paper. It was another paper. It was a legal paper he sent her to get it signed. It wasn't that she was to open the trunk and get anything out of the trunk. I was all worked up about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I assume the trunk disappeared along with her?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they know where she got off the train?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Only what they'd heard. Only what he'd told her to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But there was no record from any of the railway stations that no one saw? And they couldn't account for her trunk?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No, nothing. None of her belongings. Nothing was accounted for. I was, I know Clara used to say that Oh, they'd gone, different ones of them had gone so many places to, you know, when a body is found. And they went and examined the bodies. Even her mother had gone to her times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you go by yourself, Mother?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: She said she was afraid that it was a lead and if she let it go, you know, nobody would do it. That's the time she got help. And she just went with some friend, got some friends over there. And she checked on several, I don't know how many. Because they do find them. They're always finding them out there on the coast, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it go on for many years, their search? Did it continue for a long time?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yes, but then it wasn't a systematic search. Their little searches, you know, didn't amount to much. But Ralph had gone to Chicago, Kansas City, St. Louis. And now, I don't know. He was going, Clara said he was going to go to New Orleans, but I don't know that he did. But he did go into their departments of missing persons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they feel that she was dead?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No, they don't tell you whether they feel or not, you know. It isn't their business to feel, and so they don't tell you anything.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No, I mean her family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did her family assume that that's what had happened and not that perhaps she had just gone off with him and they disappeared together?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: They took that into consideration, too. They were intelligent people. And they did think of that, but Clara didn't think so. She thought he made a way with her. And George was sure of it. He didn't think for one minute that they'd ever see her again. George.
